blocks.text: ELSEVIER

meta.docling_orig: ELSEVIER

blocks.text: Abstract

meta.docling_orig: Abstract

blocks.text: Aspect graphs (AGs) have been proposed as viewer centered representations of 3D objects, suitable for recognizing objects from single or multiple views. Many algorithms for computing the AGs of various categories of objects have been presented; however, very few AG-based recognition applications have been developed. One reason for the limited practical use of AGs is the impressive growth of the information stored as the complexity of the object increases. This may imply comparing the views of the unknown object with a very high number of stored views, and therefore a time-consuming recognition process. The purpose of this paper is to introduce a new kind of aspect graph representation, the reduced aspect graph (RAG). RAGs are suitable for topological object recognition from single or multiple views, and require less information to be stored. Therefore, the matching process is shortened.

meta.docling_orig: Aspect graphs (AGs) have been proposed as viewer centered representations of 3D objects, suitable for recognizing objects from single or multiple views. Many algorithms for computing the AGs of various categories of objects have been presented; however, very few AG-based recognition applications have been developed. One reason for the limited practical use of AGs is the impressive growth of the information stored as the complexity of the object increases. This may imply comparing the views of the unknown object with a very high number of stored views, and therefore a time-consuming recognition process. The purpose of this paper is to introduce a new kind of aspect graph representation, the reduced aspect graph (RAG). RAGs are suitable for topological object recognition from single or multiple views, and require less information to be stored. Therefore, the matching process is shortened.

blocks.text: Keywords: Line drawings; Aspect graphs; Topological matching; Reduced aspect graph

meta.docling_orig: Keywords: Line drawings; Aspect graphs; Topological matching; Reduced aspect graph

blocks.text: 1. Introduction

meta.docling_orig: 1. Introduction

blocks.text: When computer vision is aimed at recognizing known objects in a 3D environment, a popular approach consists in storing a number of possible appearances of the objects. This should allow recognition of an object by matching one or more views of the object to the stored views.

meta.docling_orig: When computer vision is aimed at recognizing known objects in a 3D environment, a popular approach consists in storing a number of possible appearances of the objects. This should allow recognition of an object by matching one or more views of the object to the stored views.

blocks.text: A notable development along this line is the aspect graph (AG), proposed first by Koenderink and van Doorn (1979). The Ag concerns views consisting of line drawings generated by projecting onto the image plane the edges of polyhedral objects, or the creases (surface normal discontinuities) or limbs (depth discontinuities) of general objects. Two ideas are at the basis of the the AG concept. The first idea is clustering the infinite possible views into a set of representative views, the aspects, according to their topological equivalence. The second idea is the topological adjacency of the aspects. The AG is a graph structure where topologically distinct views, i.e. aspects, are stored at the vertices, and the arcs connecting topologically adjacent aspects represent topological changes, or visual events, or transitions.

meta.docling_orig: A notable development along this line is the aspect graph (AG), proposed first by Koenderink and van Doorn (1979). Two ideas are at the basis of the the AG concept. The first idea is clustering the infinite possible views into a set of representative views, the aspects, according to their topological equivalence. The second idea is the topological adjacency of the aspects. The AG is a graph structure where topologically distinct views, i.e. aspects, are stored at the vertices, and the arcs connecting topologically adjacent aspects represent topological changes, or visual events, or transitions.

blocks.text: According to recent research, human perception may involve an AG-like internal representation of objects. References to neurophysiological work on this subject, as well as other topics concerning AGs, can be found in a recent paper by Seibert and Waxman (1992).

meta.docling_orig: According to recent research, human perception may involve an AG-like internal representation of objects. References to neurophysiological work on this subject, as well as other topics concerning AGs, can be found in a recent paper by Seibert and Waxman (1992).

blocks.text: Each aspect of an object corresponds to a characteristic zone of the viewing space. The graph structure of the AG is the dual graph of the partition into characteristic zones of the viewing space. If parallel aspects, obtained from parallel projections of the object, are considered, the viewing space consists of ideal points. In this case, a useful representation of the viewing space is the Gaussian sphere. Algorithms for

blocks.text: Although the bibliography on AGs today includes dozens of papers, relatively little work has been reported on AG-based recognition algorithms. In particular, very little work fully exploits the information stored, i.e. aspects and transitions. This information suggests a multiple views recognition approach, where we match a sequence of aspects and transitions of the unknown object to the stored information.

This information suggests a multiple views recognition approach, where we match a sequence of aspects and transitions of the unknown object to the stored information.

blocks.text: An important drawback of the AG approach is the number of aspects and transitions that must be stored, even for relatively simple objects. Let us consider for instance polyhedral convex objects with n faces. If the planes supporting the faces are in general position (i.e., the intersection of each triplet of planes is a point, and all these points are different), the number of perspective aspects is

blocks.text: is the number of zones in which a hyperspace with d dimensions is divided by n hyperplanes, for n> d (see (Gruenbaum, 1967, p. 393)). The numbers of aspects and transitions of the parallel AG are 2+n(n+1) and 2n(nt1), respectively. For instance, a simple convex polyhedron with ten faces has a perspective AG with 175 aspects and 450 transitions. If the faces are not in general position, the above formulae supply upper bounds. Formulae giving the number of aspects of the case of pairs of parallel faces are reported by Laurentini (1990). The numbers of aspects can sharply increase with n for concave polyhedra. Worst case polyhedral scenes, such that nodes and arcs of the parallel and perspective AG are O(n°)

blocks.text: 2. Recognition through topological matching of multiple views

meta.docling_orig: 2. Recognition through topological matching of multiple views

blocks.text: The aspect graph idea is essentially topological. Although some researchers also store metrical information at the nodes of the aspect graph, for instance Chakravarty and Freeman (1982), in many papers an aspect is topologically defined as the graph structure of a representative line drawing. Referring to polyhedra, the arcs of this graph structure are labeled with the names of the edges of the polyhedron (Gigus et al., 1991), or marks indicating convex, concave or occluding edges (Gigus and Malik, 1990). Labeling the arcs of the graph structure of a line drawing with the names of the corresponding edges of the polyhedron is an implicit way for specifying also the labels of the arcs of the AG, i.e. the visual events.

meta.docling_orig: The aspect graph idea is essentially topological. Although some researchers also store metrical information at the nodes of the aspect graph, for instance Chakravarty and Freeman (1982), in many papers an aspect is topologically defined as the graph structure of a representative line drawing. Referring to polyhedra, the arcs of this graph structure are labeled with the names of the edges of the polyhedron (Gigus et al., 1991), or marks indicating convex, concave or occluding edges (Gigus and Malik, 1990). Labeling the arcs of the graph structure of a line drawing with the names of the corresponding edges of the polyhedron is an implicit way for specifying also the labels of the arcs of the AG, i.e. the visual events.

blocks.text: Recognition through a topological match of unknown object images and stored aspects appears to be a natural use of AGs. Should a topological match be insufficient for full identification, it could be followed by the analysis of some other feature of the unknown object.

meta.docling_orig: Recognition through a topological match of unknown object images and stored aspects appears to be a natural use of AGs. Should a topological match be insufficient for full identification, it could be followed by the analysis of some other feature of the unknown object.

blocks.text: However, it is easy to see that in many cases the information stored in an AG is redundant for this purpose. For instance, let us consider the topological recognition of a simple polyhedron, a tetrahedron, using its AG. The AG of a tetrahedron is shown in Fig. 1. In this case, the parallel and perspective AGs are coincident, and there are 14 aspects. Since we deal with a convex polyhedron, we can specify the aspects as the sets of visible faces. If we consider, from a topological point of view, a line drawing extracted from an image of the tetrahedon, it is clear that we are not able to tell one face from the other (for a regular tetrahedron this happens even if we use metrical information). Let us suppose that the current view of the

blocks.text: tetrahedron to be recognized supplies a line drawing like that shown in Fig. 2(a), and let us compare its topological structure with the aspects of the AG. We find four aspects which match this line drawing, that is, the four aspects containing three faces. Even if we use a sequence of aspects and transitions extracted from multiple images, this ambiguity cannot be resolved. By suitably displacing the viewpoint with respect to the object, we can obtain a transition toward a line drawing like that in Fig. 2(b). From this, displacing the viewpoint again, we can obtain a transition either toward a line drawing like (c) or again like (a), and so on. It is easy to see that any such sequence obtained from the object to be recognized matches several sequences of aspects and transitions of the AG, starting from any of the four aspects containing three faces. The example shows that the topological information stored in the AG of the tetrahedron is redundant: it can be reduced to the following:

blocks.text: 3. The reduced aspect graph

meta.docling_orig: 3. The reduced aspect graph

blocks.text: The example discussed in the previous section suggests the idea of reduced aspect graph (RAG) of a polyhedron. The RAG can be obtained as follows from the full AG. Consider any pair of nodes in AG and merge them together if the aspects they are labeled with are topologically equivalent. The arcs incident at the two original nodes become arcs incident at the unique resulting node. If two arcs joining the same nodes are obtained, delete one of them. Each aspect of the RAG represents a category of topologically equivalent aspects of AG. The RAG nodes must be labeled with a topological description of the aspect without reference to the edges or faces of the originating polyhedron. A description of aspect suitable for topological matching can be given in terms of adjacent polygons (Laurentini, 1993).

meta.docling_orig: The example discussed in the previous section suggests the idea of reduced aspect graph (RAG) of a polyhedron. The RAG can be obtained as follows from the full AG. Each aspect of the RAG represents a category of topologically equivalent aspects of AG. The RAG nodes must be labeled with a topological description of the aspect without reference to the edges or faces of the originating polyhedron. A description of aspect suitable for topological matching can be given in terms of adjacent polygons (Laurentini, 1993).

blocks.text: It is clear that the reduced aspect graph has the following properties:

meta.docling_orig: It is clear that the reduced aspect graph has the following properties:

blocks.text: (a) for each aspect A in AG there is one aspect RA topologically equivalent in RAG;

meta.docling_orig: (a) for each aspect A in AG there is one aspect RA topologically equivalent in RAG;

blocks.text: (b) for each transition connecting two aspects A' and A" in AG, there is in RAG a transition connecting the aspects of the topologically equivalent pair RA' and RA".

meta.docling_orig: (b) for each transition connecting two aspects A' and A" in AG, there is in RAG a transition connecting the aspects of the topologically equivalent pair RA' and RA".

blocks.text: Let us now determine the RAG for some simple polyhedra. Consider first a pentahedron. If the planes supporting the faces are in general position, from formulae (1) and (2) of Section 2 we obtain that the perspective AG has 25 nodes and 50 edges. The complete AG is shown in Fig. 3.

meta.docling_orig: Let us now determine the RAG for some simple polyhedra. Consider first a pentahedron. If the planes supporting the faces are in general position, from formulae (1) and (2) of Section 2 we obtain that the perspective AG has 25 nodes and 50 edges. The complete AG is shown in Fig. 3.

blocks.text: From this AG, by merging together the topologically equivalent aspects, we obtain the RAG shown in Fig. 4. In this figure, the aspects relative to each node have been specified by one representative line drawing. The RAG contains eight aspects and nine transitions, a substantial reduction with respect to the complete AG.

meta.docling_orig: From this AG, by merging together the topologically equivalent aspects, we obtain the RAG shown in Fig. 4. In this figure, the aspects relative to each node have been specified by one representative line drawing. The RAG contains eight aspects and nine transitions, a substantial reduction with respect to the complete AG.

blocks.text: An even larger reduction can be obtained for nongeneral polyhedra. Let us consider a cube. Its perspective AG (which is coincident with the parallel AG) contains 26 aspects, as can be obtained from the formulae giving the number of aspects for the case of polyhedra with pairs of parallel faces (Laurentini, 1990). An image of the complete AG of a cube can be found in the survey paper by Besl and Jain (1985) on three-dimensional object recognition. It is easy to see that the RAG of the cube contains only three aspects and two transitions, as shown in Fig. 5.

meta.docling_orig: An even larger reduction can be obtained for nongeneral polyhedra. Let us consider a cube. Its perspective AG (which is coincident with the parallel AG) contains 26 aspects, as can be obtained from the formulae giving the number of aspects for the case of polyhedra with pairs of parallel faces (Laurentini, 1990). An image of the complete AG of a cube can be found in the survey paper by Besl and Jain (1985) on three-dimensional object recognition. It is easy to see that the RAG of the cube contains only three aspects and two transitions, as shown in Fig. 5.

blocks.text: 4. The discriminating power of AG and RAG

meta.docling_orig: 4. The discriminating power of AG and RAG

blocks.text: We have seen that any sequence of aspects and transitions which can be found in the regular AG of a polyhedron can be also found in its RAG. Should we conclude that all the topological information of an AG is resumed by the corresponding RAG? Actually, this is not the case, as we will show with the following example.

meta.docling_orig: We have seen that any sequence of aspects and transitions which can be found in the regular AG of a polyhedron can be also found in its RAG. Should we conclude that all the topological information of an AG is resumed by the corresponding RAG? Actually, this is not the case, as we will show with the following example.

blocks.text: Let us derive the RAGs of both P and P'. It is easy to see that they are equal. In both cases, the aspects adjacent to the aspect with five faces are topologically equivalent, and thus are reduced to one aspect in the RAG (see Fig. 7).

blocks.text: In conclusion, we have shown that two polyhedra having different AGs could have the same RAG, and therefore the AG could have a greater capability than the RAG for discriminating objects topologically. However, it should be observed that this capability cannot be exploited under our recognition paradigm, consisting of : (i) extracting a sequence of aspects and transitions from an image of the object in relative motion with respect to the viewpoint; (ii) topologically matching this sequence to the stored information. In the example presented, for telling P from P', we should be able to explore the full AG of the unknown object. In particular, we should be able to find how many topologically equivalent aspects are adjacent to the aspect with five faces. For performing such tasks the viewpoint should be driven by some active algorithm based on metric properties of the object, in contrast with the simple topological approach assumed.

meta.docling_orig: In conclusion, we have shown that two polyhedra having different AGs could have the same RAG, and therefore the AG could have a greater capability than the RAG for discriminating objects topologically. However, it should be observed that this capability cannot be exploited under our recognition paradigm, consisting of : (i) extracting a sequence of aspects and transitions from an image of the object in relative motion with respect to the viewpoint; (ii) topologically matching this sequence to the stored information. In the example presented, for telling P from P', we should be able to explore the full AG of the unknown object. In particular, we should be able to find how many topologically equivalent aspects are adjacent to the aspect with five faces. For performing such tasks the viewpoint should be driven by some active algorithm based on metric properties of the object, in contrast with the simple topological approach assumed.

blocks.text: 5. Discussion

meta.docling_orig: 5. Discussion

blocks.text: We have introduced a new kind of aspect graph representation, the reduced aspect graph, which is suitable for the topological identification of polyhe-

meta.docling_orig: We have introduced a new kind of aspect graph representation, the reduced aspect graph, which is suitable for the topological identification of polyhe-

blocks.text: dral objects from multiple views. The main feature of the RAG is that any sequence of aspects and transitions which can be found in the full AG of a polyhedron can be also found in its RAG.

meta.docling_orig: dral objects from multiple views. The main feature of the RAG is that any sequence of aspects and transitions which can be found in the full AG of a polyhedron can be also found in its RAG.

blocks.text: Although the examples presented in this paper refer to convex polyhedra, the idea of reduced aspect graph can be extended to general polyhedra or even to objects with curved surfaces. The rationale of the RAG idea is that it requires storing a reduced number of views with respect to the AG, and therefore the identification of an object can be performed with a reduced number of comparisons. A substantial reduction of the information stored has been verified for some simple polyhedra.

meta.docling_orig: Although the examples presented in this paper refer to convex polyhedra, the idea of reduced aspect graph can be extended to general polyhedra or even to objects with curved surfaces. The rationale of the RAG idea is that it requires storing a reduced number of views with respect to the AG, and therefore the identification of an object can be performed with a reduced number of comparisons. A substantial reduction of the information stored has been verified for some simple polyhedra.

blocks.text: The RAG idea raises a number of problems. First observe that the algorithm described in Section 3 for computing the RAG requires O(m?) comparisons for an AG with m aspects. This means O(n'8) comparisons for the perpective RAG of a non-convex polyhedron. Thus it should be only considered an ideal procedure, and in practice more efficient algorithms are needed. A possible approach consists of modifying an algorithm for computing the full AG, like the algorithm of Gigus et al. (1991) for computing the parallel AG of general polyhedra. Each new aspect generated by this algorithm should be compared with the previously generated aspects; many comparisons could be pruned using some suitable data organization.

meta.docling_orig: The RAG idea raises a number of problems. First observe that the algorithm described in Section 3 for computing the RAG requires O(m?) comparisons for an AG with m aspects. This means O(n'8) comparisons for the perpective RAG of a non-convex polyhedron. Thus it should be only considered an ideal procedure, and in practice more efficient algorithms are needed. A possible approach consists of modifying an algorithm for computing the full AG, like the algorithm of Gigus et al. (1991) for computing the parallel AG of general polyhedra. Each new aspect generated by this algorithm should be compared with the previously generated aspects; many comparisons could be pruned using some suitable data organization.

blocks.text: For a better understanding of the advantages provided by the RAG it would be important to derive the average and worst-case complexities of the re- duced aspect graph. These problems, though restricted to convex polyhdra, do not appear to have trivial solutions.
